content.text: A side effect is any action a function takes that extends beyond computing and returning a value — any observable change to the world outside the function's own scope. In React, the render function is expected to be
: given the same props and state, it should always produce the same JSX and touch nothing else. Side effects violate this contract, which is why React provides
to quarantine them: run after render, not during.

attrs.alt: Pure render activities vs side effects that belong in useEffect

content.text: Pure Functions and Why React Requires Them

A pure function has two properties: its output depends only on its inputs (deterministic), and calling it produces no observable changes beyond returning the value (no side effects). React's render model relies on purity so it can safely re-render components at any time — for error recovery, performance optimization, or concurrent rendering — without unintended consequences.
If a component performs a side effect directly during render — writing to

— that effect runs every time the component renders. In concurrent mode, React may speculatively render a component multiple times before committing to the DOM, or abandon a render halfway. Side effects in the render body would fire inconsistently, leading to duplicate network requests, incorrect data, or corrupted external state.
A side effect in the React context is any interaction with the world outside the component's own render output. The canonical categories:
Directly reading or writing to the DOM is a side effect. Changing
, measuring an element's bounding rect, or initializing a third-party widget — all of these bypass React's virtual DOM and must happen after React has committed its own changes to the real DOM.

Fetching data from an API, opening a WebSocket connection, or sending analytics events are side effects because they interact with systems outside the component. These operations take time and can be cancelled — both properties that make them wrong to perform during the synchronous render phase.

is a side effect. The timer callback runs independently of React's render cycle. If registered during render, it would fire even after the component unmounts, potentially calling state setters on a no-longer-rendered component.

), an observable, a store, or a real-time data source is a side effect. Subscriptions need matching cleanup — failing to unsubscribe when the component unmounts leaks memory and can produce state updates on components that no longer exist.
, writing to a log, or reporting to an analytics service are side effects because they persist state or observations beyond the component's React tree.
What Is NOT a Side Effect
By contrast, the following are NOT side effects and are safe to do during render:

content: : filtering a list, sorting, computing totals — pure transformations on existing data
: building the React element tree is pure — no external mutation
: formatting dates, parsing strings, math operations
: memoizing the result of a pure computation — the factory must itself be pure
during render is technically impure but benign in React's model

content.text: React commits the DOM update first, then fires effects. This sequencing means effects always see the committed DOM. The browser may or may not have painted before the effect runs —
runs after DOM mutation but before paint (synchronous). For most effects, the asynchronous version is correct because it does not block the visual update.

React's strict mode double-invokes the render function in development to surface hidden side effects. If a component has a side effect in the render body, it will run twice per mount, making the bug visible before production. This is intentional — it is easier to find a duplicate network request in development than to debug a state corruption in production.
Every effect that opens a resource must close it. Network requests should be cancellable (via an
or a cancellation flag). Subscriptions must be unsubscribed. Timers must be cleared. DOM nodes modified outside React must be restored. This cleanup responsibility is encoded in
's return value — the function you return from the effect is called when the component unmounts or before the effect re-runs.
Not every side effect belongs in
. Event handlers are the natural home for user-triggered effects: a form submission that posts to an API, a button click that writes to localStorage, or a drag that updates a third-party canvas.
is for effects triggered by rendering — the component appearing, a prop changing, state updating.
A quick test for whether something is a side effect:
could React run this function twice without changing anything visible to the user?
If yes, it is pure. If no — because the second run would send two API requests, register two event listeners, or set the document title twice — it is a side effect and belongs in
Fetch data when a prop changes
Post a form on button click
Side Effects and the React Lifecycle

React separates the application lifecycle into two distinct phases. The render phase is a pure computation: React calls your component function, diffs the output with the previous virtual DOM, and determines what DOM changes are needed. The commit phase applies those changes. Effects run
the commit phase, ensuring that the DOM is already in the correct state when an effect executes.
The ordering guarantee is important: your effect always sees a DOM that matches the render output it was triggered by. An effect triggered by render N never sees DOM from render N+1.
Common Mistakes: Side Effects in Render

Placing side effects directly in the component body is a frequent source of bugs. React may call your component function multiple times (strict mode, concurrent features, React DevTools), so any side effect in the render body may fire an unpredictable number of times.
The pattern is always the same: move the side effect into a useEffect callback, add a dependency array, and return a cleanup function if the effect acquires a resource.
Both hooks run after the commit phase, but at different moments in the browser's rendering pipeline. Choosing the wrong one causes visual glitches or wasted work.
Network requests, subscriptions, analytics — anything that doesn't need to block the browser from painting
After DOM mutations but before paint (synchronous)
Measuring DOM nodes, imperatively positioning tooltips or popovers, any case where a delayed read would cause a flash of wrong position
only when the effect must read or write the DOM before the browser paints and the standard effect would produce a visual flash.
Because React may run your effect cleanup and re-run the effect (strict mode, fast refresh, concurrent features), a well-designed effect must be
to multiple invocations. This means each effect run should produce the same observable outcome regardless of how many times it executes.

Re-running it leaves the system in the same state as running it once. An effect that fetches data and stores it in state is idempotent because the final state value is the same no matter how many fetches completed. An effect that appends a row to a database is NOT idempotent unless it also checks for duplicates.
flag pattern ensures that a stale async callback cannot update state after the effect has been cleaned up. This protects against race conditions when

A side effect is any action that reaches beyond a function's return value and changes the observable state of the world — the DOM, a server, a timer, a subscription, or external storage. React's render model requires purity: given the same inputs, always produce the same JSX, nothing more. Side effects are delegated to
so they run after React's commit phase, not during the speculative, potentially-repeated render phase. Every side effect that opens a resource has a corresponding obligation to clean it up.

content: : any action beyond returning a value — DOM mutation, network call, timer, subscription, storage write
: same props + state → always same JSX, no external changes; React can safely re-render at any time
: concurrent mode may render speculatively; side effects in render would fire inconsistently
runs after commit: effects always see the committed DOM and do not block the browser paint
double-invokes renders to surface hidden side effects in the render body
: every effect that opens a resource must close it in the returned cleanup function
are the right place for user-triggered effects;
